summary: sensivity ships obfuscated game-cheat loader with auto-persistence and HWID exfiltration
details: On require/import of the package main (launcher.js), the loader synchronously executes two hostile actions on the installer's Windows host. First, it shells out to PowerShell to write an HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run entry named 'OneDriveUpdate' that points at a sibling VBS (OneDrive.Standalone.Updater.vbs) wrapping `wscript.exe` to relaunch `node launcher.js` hidden at every user logon — the Run-key name and VBS filename both impersonate Microsoft OneDrive to evade casual inspection. Second, launcher.js reads server.obf.js from disk and passes its contents to top-level eval(). server.obf.js is heavily obfuscator.io-packed (397-entry rotated _0x5a34 string array, RC4+base64 string decoder, control-flow flattening) and implements a socket.io control channel plus an HWID routine that reads HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Cryptography!MachineGuid via PowerShell, combines it with os.hostname() and the C: volume serial, SHA-256 hashes it, and POSTs {key, hwid, nonce, app, version} to an RC4-decoded remote license endpoint. The package also ships sens.node, a 6.8 MB Windows PE native addon whose strings ('Teleport', 'LeftFoot', 'LeftHand', 'Head') and accompanying public/index.html UI ('Start Cheat', Aimbot/Triggerbot/Silent Aim/ESP buttons) identify it as an external game-cheat / aimbot binary; the published npm description ('Sensivity Control Panel') does not disclose this. Any developer who installs or transitively pulls this package gets a cheat binary dropped, auto-persisted under a Microsoft-impersonating Run key, and a stable hardware fingerprint sent to an attacker-controlled endpoint when the panel is used.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
